notes: Dual send: web form + follow direct contact if identified. Named contact if available. National network reach. 50+ affiliated programs. High mission alignment with ELITE threat model.
400+ affiliated programs nationally. Network multiplier = automatic Factor 3 point. Contact: communications or national office. Slow institutional review cycle possible but very high leverage.
Named contact: Thaís Silva-Marques (communications). High-volume client base in Texas — highest enforcement zone. Fastest likely converter among 1A orgs due to operational urgency.

Named contact: Kemi Bello (advocacy). Publications/practitioner channel is the right entry point. May want to host as a resource on their site. Primary-source structure highly relevant to practitioner publications.
Mass Defense Committee. Signal expertise — will understand threat model depth. Citable sourcing is strong draw. Consider reaching tech-and-law committee directly.
Mid-Atlantic immigrant advocacy. Strong community education infrastructure. Best approached via web contact form. Program director or community education lead is right routing.
NY entry point with state chapters. Media contact is appropriate for initial outreach. Community education program infrastructure exists. Translation capacity available.
DACA-focused national network. Strong digital communications infrastructure. May amplify via social media — watch for Bitly spikes following send. Translation of materials may be feasible.

notes: Cross-border operations (Mexico + US). Organizes migrant workers. Spanish-language materials would be particularly relevant. Unique reach into population before US entry.
User to identify based on local presence. Search: [city] sanctuary network. Most have Signal groups. Contact may be Signal-first — flag for parallel Signal outreach if email available.

Reaches people in active legal jeopardy — highest-urgency population. Security culture high. Part 0 and Tier 1 checklist are the primary sections for this audience. Email may be secondary to direct network outreach.

Network infrastructure for bail fund members. Reaches member funds and affiliated organizations. Signal-first communication preferred. Bitly click spike from new geographic areas = strong Score 4 indicator.

Named contact: Rebecca Jeschke. Backup: info@eff.org. High mission alignment with ELITE threat model. Deeplinks blog potential for corpus citation. Primary-source structure is strong fit for EFF methodology.
Backup: info@cdt.org. Target: Privacy & Data team or Security & Surveillance team specifically. Policy-focused — congressional staff and researcher reach. Named team routing is Score 4 indicator.
Also: security@accessnow.org (Digital Security Helpline). Global reach; strong focus on at-risk populations including undocumented people. Helpline team would directly use corpus for client support.
London-based. Runs active data broker campaigns. Primary-source structure of threat model is highly relevant to their methodology. Data broker opt-out methodology is directly aligned.
Secure contact: tor.project@protonmail.com. Corpus recommends Tor — informing them of specific use-case threat model creates natural alignment. May publish blog post or resource link.
Backup: policy@mozilla.com. Has published surveillance threat analyses. Institutional amplification potential via blog or resource listing. Policy team is right routing.
Action-campaign focused. Best approached after larger orgs have validated — can amplify once credibility established. High social media reach. Bitly spike likely if they share.
Coalition partner potential — connects digital rights with labor and immigration advocacy. Dual-track amplification to labor and immigration communities.
Named contact: Albert Fox Cahn (Executive Director). NYC/state-level ICE surveillance focus — directly relevant to ELITE New York deployment. High specificity match with corpus threat model.
Named contact: Jeff Chester (Executive Director). Tracks commercial data broker misuse specifically — corpus directly addresses this. High confidence of reading beyond subject line if reached.
FOIA-intensive organization. Corpus cites FOIA-sourced contracts — natural credibility fit. May publish analysis or resource listing. FOIA methodology alignment is strong hook.
Grassroots; relevant to warrantless data broker purchase angle. Lower institutional amplification than EFF/CDT but activist network reach. Social media amplification potential.
Director: Lorrie Faith Cranor. 50+ faculty, 100+ graduate students. Usable privacy research lineage — corpus fits research citation and teaching use cases. Semester cycle: extend threshold to Day 28.
Executive Director: Ann Cleaveland. Backup: shanti@berkeley.edu. Funds applied security research with policy implications. May consider corpus for funded research context.
Internet Policy Research Initiative. Also: CSAIL Security Seminar. Theoretical + applied crypto with policy reach. Semester cycle: extend threshold to Day 28.
Human-centered security research; active applied program. Allen School contact form backup. Research on at-risk populations directly aligns. Semester cycle: extend threshold to Day 28.
Note: Stanford Internet Observatory ended 2024. Cyber Policy Center continues. Policy focus means faster conversion timeline than pure research programs. Named faculty contact preferred.
Student group RPISEC engaged with community outreach — may accelerate initial response compared to pure faculty contact. Consider dual outreach to faculty and student group.

Named contact: Christopher Bavitz (Managing Director, Cyberlaw Clinic). Clinic has active caseloads — faster conversion than research faculty. Immigration law intersection strong. Likely Score 3 within 14 days.
Institute for Information Security & Privacy. Strong applied security program. Not in original list but one of strongest US applied security programs. Semester cycle: extend threshold to Day 28.
Cybersecurity & Privacy Institute. Applied crypto and privacy-preserving systems. Corpus methodology aligns with their research standards. Semester cycle: extend threshold to Day 28.
CFP: cfp@defcon.org. DEF CON 34 CFP opens late 2025. Forum: forum.defcon.org. Peer-to-peer framing required — treat as reviewer, not audience. Bitly click without reply is normal for this sector.
Annual Congress in Hamburg. Submission portal: events.ccc.de. Strong civil liberties tradition; ELITE threat model directly fits CCC interest areas. Response highly variable.
CFP opens annually January. Corporate/government audience; reaches CISO community. Understanding ELITE data purchases is relevant to enterprise security professionals. Long response latency expected.
Washington DC–based. Accepts community talk proposals. Relevant for source protection and opsec content. Smaller scale but influential DC-area security community.

Approach via published contact info (Mastodon, security.txt, conference networking). Target 3–5 researchers publishing on surveillance tech or law enforcement data pipelines. Each researcher is a separate row if contacted.
Also: press@freedom.press. Builds SecureDrop — corpus recommends it. Natural partner. High probability of republication or resource citation. FISA 702 deadline is strong hook.

NICAR conference covers digital security tools. Missouri School of Journalism base. Training channel is primary target — corpus could integrate as training resource. Named contact in digital security preferred.
International focus but digital security content translates globally. Digital safety team is right routing target. Source protection angle is primary hook.
